column102: Do you have any remarks or comments?

column44: Do not understand the comparison questions, so marked equally in all cases
column102: Assessing safety from microscopic traffic simulation is questionable since the models are "safe by design" and commonly do not capture human driving errors or in a simplistic way. Hence using surrugate safety metrics as TTC, PET from traffic ssmulation need to be done with great care. This applies even more to e.g. bicycle traffic which requires further model development before it can be stated to represent bike behaviour.

column56: I'm British, so roundabouts are more familiar than 4-way stops

column85: Unless I miss the point, your questions are quite surprising to me: in my opinion, the "static" configuration of the network (two or three lanes, on-ramp or off-ramp) is not the main criterion for the confidence one may give to a simulation run. There are plenty of factors such as the software tool, the type of model (including lane changing model and gap acceptance model), the chosen parameters, the replications and finaly the demand level (resulting in free-flow / congested condition) -which is somehow the object of your last question but it mixes up different things: network configuration VS demand.

column73: This question was really difficult to understand and I am unsure why a ranking question wasn't asked directly

column15: I have no experience of micro simulation models under degraded situations. I considered the cloudy state as a slight decrease in luminosity.

column102: I worked on surrogate safety measures for drivers and other road users, but not on traffic simulation. Despite that, doing the comparisons in the questionnaire was not easy.

column85: divergent -> diverge ; convergent -> merge en anglais
column102: rappelle tes indicateurs critiques avant qu'on commence à répondre aux comparaisons des différentes configurations

column44: I am not sure to understand all the objectives of the questions. However, to my opinion, some informations are missing. The most important of them is the value of the time step. Second, the representation of the bicycle and the motocycle in the simulation, e.g. the occupied space, it is a box or an ellipse?
column102: Instead of asking to the participants their level of confidence in indicators, you could list the indicators and ask to the participant if they know them and if they already used them

column27: These questions are hard to understand

column15: Adding unusual signs to represent lights and rain makes it less realistic compared to sunny scenario

column73: In general, multimodal dynamics influence the way risk indicators are computed. Therefore, detail modelling on hybrid interactions are important to determine factors. Car following models simplify this interaction by assuming full observability conditions of lead-follower behaviour or mixed lanes. Some of the aspects that may contribute to improve risk indications is proper dynamics of the individual + the way it interacts with its environment (as en example it does not take the same time to accelerate/break a bicycle vs tram) secondly, infrastructure play a role (bicycles can go in the middle of cars, trams may have priority on traffic lights)

column97: For up and down slopes, it depends a lot on the calibration: when you drive a slope up, your speed is reduced and you can accept a shorter gap with the leader since gravity help you to quicker reduce your speed

column27: Windy situations are poorly studied in literature. We can find references for rain or snow, but their modelling is usually challenging even if models exist for rainy situations

column102: I don't know what TTC is.

column85: Very interesting survey. Some of the questions could have been more carefully designed.
